coredata.dc:title: Paper-based electrochemiluminescence immunodevice for carcinoembryonic antigen using nanoporous gold-chitosan hybrids and graphene quantum dots functionalized Au@Pt
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Li
Li, Weiping
Ma, Chao
Yang, Hongmei
Ge, Shenguang
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive 3D origami device combined with electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor was introduced for sensitive point-of-care testing of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA). This immunodevice was developed based on nanoporous gold/chitosan modified paper working electrode (NGC-PWE) as sensor platform and green-luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles (GQDs/Au@Pt) as signal label. Firstly, the origami device was fabricated by directly screen-printing electrodes on wax-patterned pure cellulose paper. Secondly, the NGC hybrids were prepared and introduced for binding of primary antibody with high loading amount. The ECL nanoprobe (Ab2/GQDs/Au@Pt) was designed by covalent assembly of signal antibody (Ab2) on GQDs tagged Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles. After a sandwich-type immunoreaction, the GQDs/Au@Pt labels were captured onto the NGC-PWE surface. The amplified ECL signals were achieved by efficient catalysis of the glucose oxidase (GOx) towards the oxidation of glucose to in situ generate H2O2 as coreactant and the enhancement of Au@Pt to the ECL reaction of GQDs-H2O2. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the proposed strategy exhibited excellent high sensitivity for the determination of CEA ranging from 1.0pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1 with the detection limit of 0.6pgmL−1. The assay results of CEA in clinical serum samples were in a good agreement with the commercial ECL single-analyte tests. The proposed enhanced ECL immunosensor may become a promising technique for detection of other tumor markers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
3D origami immunodevice
Screen-printing electrodes
Point-of-care testing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the 3D origami ECL device
Preparation of this 3D origami ECL immunodevice
Characterization of this 3D origami ECL immunodevice
ECL emission on 3D origami ECL immunodevice
Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the immunodevice
Application in analysis of serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation, size, shape, and detection of this 3D origami ECL device. (A) Paper sheets were firstly patterned in bulk using a wax printer; (B) One side of the origami ECL device without the screen-printed electrodes; (C) The reverse side of (B) with the screen-printed electrodes; (D) After modification, the origami ECL device was integrated with a transparent device-holder, and glucose was added to trigger the ECL reaction.

The schematic representation of the fabrication procedure for this 3D origami ECL immunodevice. (1) Bare PWE; (2) NGC modified PWE; (3) after immobilization with McAb1 and BSA; (4) after capture and washing; (5) after incubation with signal antibodies and washing.

SEM (A) and TEM (B) images of Au@Pt; (C) TEM image of the GQDs. Inset: size distributions of GQDs; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra Au@Pt (a), GQDs (b), McAb2 (c), GOx (d), GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt (e). Inset: photograph of GQDs taken under UV light.

(A) SEM image of the boundary of the wax-pattern: pure cellulose paper (left) and wax-printed paper (right); (B) SEM images of nanoporous gold; (C) NGC hybrids; (D) EIS of bare PWE (a), NGC-PWE, (b) McAb1 modified NGC-PWE (c), BSA/McAb1 modified NGC-PWE (d), Ag/BSA/McAb1 modified NGC-PWE (e), and GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt/Ag/BSA/McAb1 modified NGC-PWE, (f) in 0.01molL−1 PBS (2.5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1M KCl, pH 7.4).

(A) ECL-potential curves in (a) 5.0ngmL−1 CEA, (b) 0.1ngmL−1 CEA and (c) without CEA in a potential range between 1.0V and −0.2V. (B) ECL profiles of the immuosensor using NPG modified PWE (curve b), CHIT modified PWE (curve c) and NGC modified PWE (curve a) in pH 7.4 PBS containing 0.1M glucose.
Effect of (A) pH of detection solution, (B) incubation time on ECL intensities in the presence of 10ngmL−1 CEA, where n =10 for each point.

(A) ECL emission of the 3D origami ECL immunodevice under continuous CVs for 10 cycles (using 5ngmL−1 CEA as an example). (B) Relationship between ECL signal and antigen concentration for CEA. Inset: logarithmic calibration curve.
The results of comparing with other immunoassay biosensing systems.
Assay results of human serum samples by the proposed and reference method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average value of six successive determinations.

head.ce:title: Paper-based electrochemiluminescence immunodevice for carcinoembryonic antigen using nanoporous gold-chitosan hybrids and graphene quantum dots functionalized Au@Pt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive 3D origami device combined with electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor was introduced for sensitive point-of-care testing of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA). This immunodevice was developed based on nanoporous gold/chitosan modified paper working electrode (NGC-PWE) as sensor platform and green-luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles (GQDs/Au@Pt) as signal label. Firstly, the origami device was fabricated by directly screen-printing electrodes on wax-patterned pure cellulose paper. Secondly, the NGC hybrids were prepared and introduced for binding of primary antibody with high loading amount. The ECL nanoprobe (Ab2/GQDs/Au@Pt) was designed by covalent assembly of signal antibody (Ab2) on GQDs tagged Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles. After a sandwich-type immunoreaction, the GQDs/Au@Pt labels were captured onto the NGC-PWE surface. The amplified ECL signals were achieved by efficient catalysis of the glucose oxidase (GOx) towards the oxidation of glucose to in situ generate H2O2 as coreactant and the enhancement of Au@Pt to the ECL reaction of GQDs-H2O2. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the proposed strategy exhibited excellent high sensitivity for the determination of CEA ranging from 1.0pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1 with the detection limit of 0.6pgmL−1. The assay results of CEA in clinical serum samples were in a good agreement with the commercial ECL single-analyte tests. The proposed enhanced ECL immunosensor may become a promising technique for detection of other tumor markers.

ce:section.ce:para: Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μ-PADs) have appeared as a very promising platform for point-of-care (POC) diagnosis in the last five years [1–3]. As an analytical support, paper, being low cost, highly abundant, and biodegradable as well as being easy to use, transport, and print, has excellent chemical compatibility with many applications. Importantly, μ-PADs can be fabricated in bulk using less expensive means, including cutting, plasma etching, inkjet etching wax-printing and photolithography [4–12]. Among these fabrication methods, wax-printing is the cheapest and most easily implemented means of mass production available, and has recently been shown to be very effective in the production of μ-PADs at minute cost. Inspired by this simple technique, many groups have paid great efforts to the development of μ-PADs, including fabrication methods for μ-PADs [13,14], functionalization of μ-PADs [15,16], and analytical methods on μ-PADs [17,18]. The success of μ-PADs arises from three main factors: low cost, ease of operation, and ability to function without any external pumps.

Rapid, quantitative and sensitive detection of tumor markers play an important role in early detection and therapy monitoring [19]. Immunoassay based on the highly specific interaction between antigen and antibody is one of the most important methods for the specific analysis of those cancer biomarkers [20]. Conventional detection methods for cancer biomarkers, such as, chemiluminescence [21], mass spectrometric immunoassays [22], electrophoretic [23], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [24] and radioimmunoassay [25], are complicated, time-consuming, tedious, expensive, and labor-intensive. Moreover, these methods are not suitable for POC diagnosis. As an alternative to the conventional immunoassay procedures, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has received considerable attention due to its intrinsic advantages such as fast response time, low-cost instrumentation, and high detection sensitivity [26].

Signal amplification is the most popular strategy that has been extensively used for the development of highly sensitive ECL immunoassay method. The use of nanomaterials as signal amplifiers is of particular interest in biosensor design, due to their outstanding electronic and biocompatible performance. Nanoporous gold (NPG) [27] has attracted considerable attention in recent years due to its high surface-to-volume reaction, high in-plane conductivity, and good stability. In particularly, NPG could be used in multiple-label assays with glucose oxidase (GOx) to generate H2O2 as coreactant for signal amplification [28]. In addition, chitosan (CHIT), a natural biocompatible polysaccharide with amino and hydroxyl groups, has been extensively used in biosensing [29]. Therefore, in this work, NPG/CHIT (NGC) hybrids as sensor platform were promising for constructing ECL biosensor because of their good biocompatibility and high stability. On the other hand, to substitute a single label with a carrier loaded with a large number of labels is a versatile signal amplification technique widely used in ECL-based bioassay. It has been reported that 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was an eco-friendly material as well as excellent ECL labeling agents for biosensor [30,31]. However, the ECL signals of GQDs are usually low, which limits its wide application in bioassays. Bimetallic nanoparticles with core–shell structures have received tremendous research interest because of their high surface-to-volume reaction, high electrical conductivity, and good biocompatibility. Herein, we have successfully fabricated green-luminescent GQDs functionalized Au@Pt nanoparticles (GQDs/Au@Pt) as signal amplification label. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no report on the integration of NGC hybrids and GQDs/Au@Pt into μ-PADs for the quantitative analysis of cancer marker.

In this work, we successfully designed a 3D origami ECL immunodevice for sensitive carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) assay based on dual-signal amplification strategy. The prepared NGC hybrids were nanoporous, conductive, and biocompatible, creating an “ideal” porous thin-film sensor platform to improve electron transfer and absorb abundant antibodies. Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles were selected as nanocarriers for loading numerous GQDs to form GQDs tagged Au@Pt labels due to their high electrical conductivity and high surface area, and were brought onto the NGC modified paper working electrode (NGC-PWE) surface upon the completion of sandwich immunoreactions. By coupling with a simple home-made device-holder and ECL analytical technique, the prepared ECL immunodevice exhibited high sensitivity and specificity and excellent performance for highly sensitive detection of CEA in real human serum assay. This work contributes to the production of simple and sensitive GQDs-based ECL immunoassay on μ-PADs.

Human CEA, mouse monoclonal capture antibodies (McAb1) and signal antibodies (McAb2) were purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The clinical serum samples were purchased from Shandong Tumor Hospital. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), glucose oxidase (GOx), graphite powder (KS-10, 99.95%), ascorbic acid and chitosan (CHIT) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (USA). Carbon ink (ELECTRODEDAGPF-407C) and silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) ink (ELECTRODAG7019 (18DB19C)) was purchased from Acheson (Germany). Glutaraldehyde (GA, 25% aqueous solution) was purchased from AlfaAesar China Ltd. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) and dimethyl formamide (DMF) were obtained from Shanghai Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). The Au/Ag alloy (42:58wt%, 25μm thick) foils were obtained from Changshu Noble Metal Company. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water obtained from a Millipore water purification system (>18.2MΩcm, Milli-Q, Millipore) was used in all assays and solutions. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10mM) with different pH values were prepared by mixing the stock solutions of KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4) containing 0.1M glucose was used as the electrolyte in ECL analysis. The washing buffer was PBS (pH 7.4, 10.0mM) containing 0.05% (w/v) Tween-20. PBS (pH 7.4, 10.0mM) containing 0.5% (w/v) BSA and 0.5% (w/v) casein was used as blocking solution. What-man chromatography paper #1 (20.0cm×20.0cm) (pure cellulose paper) was obtained from GE Healthcare Worldwide (Pudong Shanghai, China) and used with further adjustment of size.

The ECL measurements were carried out using a flow injection luminescence analyzer (IFFM-E, Xi’an Remex Electronic Instrument High-Tech Ltd., Xi’an, China) with the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) set at 800V. Cyclic voltammetric measurements (CVs) were performed with a CHI 760D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments, Co China). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed on an IM6x electrochemical station (Zahner, Germany). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded using a QUANTA FEG 250 thermal field emission SEM (FEI Co., USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations were performed using JEOL JEM-1400 microscope (Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded on a UV-2500 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the 3D origami ECL device
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the 3D origami ECL device was similar to our previous work [32] and a detailed procedure was described below. Wax printing is rapid, inexpensive, and particularly well suited for producing large lots (hundreds to thousands) of prototype μ-PADs. Firstly, the shape of the hydrophilic working zones was designed with Adobe illustrator CS4, and corresponding wax patterns were printed onto the surface of pure paper in bulk with a wax printer (FUJIXEROX Phaser 8560DN, Japan). Then the wax-printed paper sheet was placed on a digital hot plate set at 150°C for 130s. As shown in Scheme 1A, on each origami ECL device, there were two circular paper working zones (named circle-A: 6.0mm in diameter on sample tab and circle-B: 8.0mm in diameter on auxiliary pad) for screen-printing electrodes. The electrode array consisted of a screen-printed Ag/AgCl reference electrode and carbon counter electrode on the paper auxiliary zone and a screen-printed carbon working electrode on the paper sample zone. The carbon and Ag/AgCl electrodes were screen printed directly on the paper sheet surface using carbon ink and Ag/AgCl ink, respectively, through the screen mesh. Finally, the paper sheet was cut into individual origami ECL device for further modifications (Scheme 1B and C). Between the sample tab and auxiliary pad, the unprinted line (1mm in width) was defined as fold line. After folding, the three screen-printed electrodes (working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode) would be connected once the paper electrochemical cell was filled with solution (40μL).

NPG is made by selective dissolution (dealloying) of silver from silver/gold alloy according to the reported method [33]. Dealloying was carried out in 1:1 concentrated nitric acid at a constant temperature (25°C) for 10min. After washing to neutral with Milli-Q water, the obtained nanoporous gold was dispersed into CHIT solution (0.5mgmL−1) using sonication (Kun Shan Ultrasonic Instruments Co., Ltd, KQ2200DB, 100W) for 20min to give a homogeneous solution.

The preparation processes of Au@Pt were described as follows: Initially, colloidal gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with diameter of 13nm were prepared by reducing gold chloride tetrahydrate with citric acid at 100°C for half an hour [34]. Secondly, 125mL of the AuNPs solution was mixed with 125mL H2O and heated to 100°C, and 12.5mL of 1% H2PtCl6 (20mM) was added under stirring. Afterwards, 10mL of 100mM ascorbic acid was dropped into the solution in 2min, and the reaction mixture was heated for 20min to yield the Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles.

The preparation of GQDs was similar to a previous protocol [35] with slight modifications and the procedure was described below. Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [36,37]. Briefly, GO was dissolved in DMF with the concentrations of 270mgmL−1. The GO/DMF solutions were under ultrasonication for 30min. Subsequently, the GO/DMF solutions were transferred to Teflon-sealed autoclave, maintained at 180°C for 6h and then quenched by lowering the temperature naturally. The resulting product was purified by evaporating the brown transparent suspension and redispersion in water.

For the immobilization of GQDs, briefly, 1.0mL Au@Pt and 2.0mL ethylenediamine was first dispersed in 5mL water by sonication for 1h. After stirring for 5h, the suspension was centrifuged and washed with water repeatedly for three times, and the amino-functionalized Au@Pt was obtained. Then, the resulting amino-functionalized Au@Pt was dispersed in a mixture of 1.0mL as-prepared GQDs and 1.0mL EDC (20mgmL−1). The mixed suspension was stirred at 4°C for 12h. After the activated GQDs/Au@Pt labels were thoroughly rinsed with water, they were immediately redispersed into a mixture of PBS solution (pH 7.4, 10mM) containing McAb2 (20μgmL−1), EDC (20mgmL−1) and NHS (20mgmL−1), followed by incubation at room temperature for 2h to form McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt bioconjugates. Subsequently, 1.0mL GOx (1.0mgmL−1) was added into the obtained McAb2 labeled McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt bioconjugates and incubated for 8h at 4°C to block non-specific binding sites. Finally, the obtained bioconjugates were then stored at 4°C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of this 3D origami ECL immunodevice
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 2 , the 3D origami ECL immunodevice was constructed by immobilizing McAb1 onto paper working zone through NGC coating and GA cross-linking. First, 4.0μL of NGC solution was coated on the paper working zone and dried at room temperature. After activating with 2.5% GA for 30min and washing with water, 4.0μL of McAb1 was applied to the paper working zone and incubated at room temperature for 40min. Subsequently, excess antibodies were washed with washing buffer. Then each paper working zone was blocked by adding 4.0μL of blocking solution to block possible remaining active sites against nonspecific adsorption, and allowing the paper working zone to dry for 20min at room temperature. After another washing with the washing buffer, the resulting ECL immunodevice was obtained and stored at 4°C in dry air environment prior to use.

To carry out the immunoreaction and ECL detections, the 3D origami ECL immunodevice was firstly incubated with a 4.0μL of sample solution containing different concentrations of CEA in PBS for 160s at room temperature. After washing with washing buffer to prevent nonspecific binding and achieve the best possible signal-to-background ratio, the origami ECL immunodevice was then incubated with 4.0μL of GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt bioconjugates for 160s at room temperature, followed by washing with washing buffer.

Ultimately, as shown in Scheme 1D, one sample tab was folded down below the auxiliary pad successively and clamped into the device-holder, which was comprised of two circuit boards (named Board-A and Board-B respectively below) with conductive pads on them, to fix and connect this origami ECL immunodevice to the electrochemical workstation. Then, 40μL of PBS solution (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M glucose was added through the hole on board-A. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were used to trigger the ECL reaction in the sweeping range from −0.2 to 1.0V with a scan rate of 100mVs−1 at room temperature. The ECL emission was detected by the PMT biased at 800V. The ECL signals related to CEA concentrations could be detected.

As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the SEM image displayed a type of spherical morphology and the TEM image displayed the morphology of Au@Pt nanoparticles was core-shell structure in the size 20nm with improved monodispersity. In addition, strong fluorescent GQDs were prepared by one-step solvothermal method. Fig. 1C shows the TEM image of GQDs and their size distributions (insert in Fig. 1C). Subsequently, the Au@Pt could further attach GQDs and McAb2 and GOx for the biofunctionalization of the Au@Pt.

The GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt bioconjugates were further characterized using UV–vis spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1D, a visible absorption band at around 505nm was observed for Au@Pt (curve a). The GQDs showed an obvious absorption peak at about 320nm (curve b). The prepared GQDs exhibited a green color under ultraviolet radiation (λ =365nm) (insert in Fig. 1D). Three distinct adsorption peaks from pure GOx were observed (curve d). One adsorption peak at about 276nm was corresponding to the amino acid residues in proteins (the pure GOx and McAb2 (curve c)), and two characteristic absorption peaks of the oxidized form of the pure GOx were located at about 382 and 452nm, respectively. When McAb2 together with GOx were linked to GQDs/Au@Pt, five obvious absorption peaks were observed on the resulting GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt (curve e), indicating successful binding of McAb2 and GOx onto GQDs/Au@Pt.

In this work, pure cellulose paper was used as substrate to fabricate a portable, simple POC diagnose device. The hydrophobic walls were fabricated by printing wax onto the paper surface in a high-resolution printing mode (Fig. 2A). The SEM image of NPG displayed a type of sponge-like morphology with a 15–20nm sized pore structure (Fig. 2B). After CHIT was coated on the NPG, a smooth and uniform structure was observed (Fig. 2C), indicating the attachment of CHIT on NPG film through electrostatic adsorption. Due to its high surface area-to-weight ratio, fast electron transportation and good biocompatibility, the NGC hybrids could be further used as sensor platform in bioassay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of this 3D origami ECL immunodevice
ce:section.ce:para: EIS was an effective method for monitoring the changes in the surface features of the modified PWE in the assembly process [38,39]. As shown in Fig. 2D, it was observed that the bare PWE showed a relatively small semicircle domain (curve a). After NGC was modified on PWE, a lower resistance was observed (curve b), which may be because the abundant amino groups could adsorb much more negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and could be easily reached at the PWE surface to facilitate the interfacial electron-transfer. Subsequently, the immobilization of McAb1 through GA linking generated an insulating protein layer (curve c), which increased the resistance. As BSA, Ag, and GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt could all resist the electron transfer kinetics of redox probe at the electrode interface, an increased tendency of the impedance was observed during their stepwise attachment (curves d–f), which testified to the immobilization of these substances on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL emission on 3D origami ECL immunodevice
ce:section.ce:para:
ECL behaviors of the designed immunodevice were studied in 0.1M, pH 7.4 PBS with 0.1M glucose as coreactant. As shown in Fig. 3A, one ECL wave appeared during the anodic scan (−0.2 to 1.0V) with an onset potential of 0.4V (Fig. 3A, curve b). In addition, the anodic ECL intensity of GQDs-labeled immunocomplexes increased as the concentration of CEA increased (Fig. 3A, curve a) and reached its minimum when no CEA was added into the 3D origami ECL immunodevice (Fig. 3A, curve c). The possible ECL mechanisms are as follows [40]:
(1)
Glucose +
O 2
⟶
GOx
gluconic
acid +
H 2
O 2
(2) GQDs−e− →GQDs•+
(3) H2O2 ⇌H+ +HOO−
(4) HOO− −e•− →HOO• ↔O2 •−
(5) GQDs•+ +O2 •− →GQDs*
(6) GQDs* →GQDs+ hν

To clarify the improved performance of the developed immunoassay using NGC modified PWE as sensor platform, a comparison was performed based on the shift of the ECL signals. As can be seen in Fig. 3B, when the immunodevice was incubated with different concentrations of CEA standard solutions, a increase of ECL signal could be observed on NPG modified PWE (curve b) and CHIT modified PWE (curve c), whereas an obvious increases of ECL emission appeared from NGC modified PWE (curve a). The reasons may be that the NGC modified PWE provided an excellent platform by improving electron transfer and absorbing abundant antibodies.

As shown in Fig. 4A, the ECL intensity of this 3D origami ECL immunodevice showed great dependence on the pH value of the detection solution. As well known, the highly acidic or alkaline surroundings would affect the immunoreaction of antibody-antigen, so the effect of pH has been investigated over the pH range of 6.03–8.46. With the increasing of pH from 6.03 to 7.4, the ECL intensity increased. When the pH value of the buffer solution was higher than 7.4, the ECL intensity was decreased. Accordingly, the PBS solution of pH 7.4 was chosen for further ECL detection.

The effects of incubation time on the ECL intensity of this 3D origami ECL immunodevice were investigated between 80 and 200s. With the increasing incubation time used in sandwich-type, the ECL response increased gradually and reached a plateau after 160s (Fig. 4B), indicating the maximum formation of these sandwich immunocomplexes. Thus, 160s was used as the optimal immunoreaction time.

Fig. 5A shows the ECL emission of the 3D origami immunodevice after dipping in 5ngmL−1 of CEA solution. The ECL signals were high and stable, which suggested that the immunodevice was suitable for ECL detection.

Under optimized assay conditions, this proposed 3D origami ECL immunodevice was evaluated with different concentrations of CEA in PBS. As shown in Fig. 5B, the ECL intensity of the immunodevice enhanced with the increasing concentration of analyte. The calibration plot showed a good linear relationship between the ECL intensity and the logarithm of the CEA concentration in the range from 1.0pgmL−1 to 10ngmL−1 (inset, Fig. 5B). The limit of detection at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 was 0.6pgmL−1, which was much lower than other sensors reported previously (Table 1 ). Compared with these methods, the immunosensor has a relatively large linear range and low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the immunodevice
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the immunosensor played an important role in analyzing biological samples in situ without separation. Some coexisting species including BSA (100ngmL−1), casein (100ngmL−1) and hemoglobulin (100ngmL−1) were used as the influences to assess the specificity. The immunodevice was incubated in two copies of 1.0ngmL−1 CEA aqueous solutions with and without each of the substances, respectively. No significant difference (relative standard deviation (RSD=5.6%) was observed in ECL response from the solution pair, suggesting the acceptable specificity of the immunosensor for CEA. The reproducibility of the immunodevice for CEA was investigated with intra- and interassay precision. The intra-assay precision was evaluated by assaying one CEA level for six similar measurements. The interassay precision was estimated by testing one CEA level with six immunodevices made at the same PWE independently. The intra- and interassay variation coefficients obtained from 1.0ngmL−1 CEA were 5.2% and 6.4%, respectively, indicating good precision and reproducibility. Furthermore, over 91.0% of original responses retained after 20 days of storage at 4°C for CEA, indicating the good stability of the immunodevice. In addition, the long-term stability of GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt bioconjugates was tested. Due to the biocompatibility of Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles, GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt bioconjugates could be stored for long period. When they were stored for 30 days, it remained 93.7% of initial responses. This result showed that GOx/McAb2/GQDs/Au@Pt bioconjugates exhibited good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in analysis of serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential application of this 3D origami ECL immunodevice, the sensing of CEA in human serum was carried out. Before the assay, the serum samples were diluted appropriately step by step to be in the linear range of the immunodevice. The assay results of four clinical serum samples using the proposed method were compared with reference values obtained by commercial ECL single-analyte tests (Electrochemiluminescent Analyzer, ROCHE E601, Switzerland) in Shandong Tumor Hospital. The results listed in Table 2 showed an acceptable agreement, with relative errors less than 4.65%, indicating the acceptable accuracy of the proposed method for the detection of cancer marker in clinical and POC diagnosis.

In this work, a 3D origami ECL immunodevice was fabricated for sensitive and specific POC diagnostics based on NGC modified paper working electrode as sensor platform and GQDs/Au@Pt as signal amplification label. The immobilization of antibodies on the NGC modified PWE was very useful for performing high-performance ECL immunoassay by improving electron transfer and absorbing abundant antibodies. The loading of numerous GQDs on Au@Pt produced an amplified ECL signal and further improved the sensitivity. In addition, the attached GOx kept good biological activity and exhibited efficient catalysis towards glucose to in situ generate H2O2 as coreactant. The resulting immunodevice, with high sensitivity, acceptable reproducibility and stability, will be very useful in quantitative detection of CEA .This proposed method can be expanded readily for detecting other cancer biomarkers and has the potential for reliable diagnosis of cancer and other diseases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21277058, 21175058), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diagnostics for the developing world: microfluidic paper-based analytical devices
Microfluidics: on the slope of enlightenment
Multiplex lateral-flow test strips fabricated by two-dimensional shaping
Paper-based microfluidic devices by plasma treatment
Inkjet-printed microfluidic multianalyte chemical sensing paper
Understanding wax printing: a simple micropatterning process for paper-based microfluidics
Lab-on-paper with dual electrochemical/colorimetric detection for simultaneous determination of gold and iron
Fabrication of disposable electrochemical devices using silver ink and office paper
Colorimetric sensor arrays: Interplay of geometry, substrate and immobilization
Miniaturized total chemical analysis systems: a novel concept for chemical sensing
Flash: a rapid method for prototyping paper-based microfluidic devices
Three-dimensional paper microfluidic devices assembled using the principles of origami
Inkjet printing of conjugated polymer precursors on paper substrates for colorimetric sensing and flexible electrothermochromic display
Fully enclosed microfluidic paper-based analytical devices
Inkjet printing of nanoporous gold electrode arrays on cellulose membranes for high-sensitive paper-like electrochemical oxygen sensors using ionic liquid electrolytes
Early detection: proteomic applications for the early detection of cancer
Ultrasensitive enhanced chemiluminescence enzyme-immunoassay for the determination of α-fetoprotein amplified by double-codified gold nanoparticles labels
Mass spectrometric immunoassay for quantitative determination of protein biomarker isoforms
Ultrashort separation length homogeneous electrophoretic immunoassays using on-chip discontinuous polyacrylamide gels
Development of electrogenerated chemiluminescence-based enzyme linked immunosorbent assay for sub-pM detection
Uderstanding wax printing: a simple micropatterning process for paper-based microfluidics
Low temperature CO oxidation over unsupported nanoporous gold
A 3D origami multiple electrochemiluminescence immunodevice based on a porous silver-paper electrode and multi-labeled nanoporous gold–carbon spheres
Magnetic beads-based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for determination of cancer markers using quantum dot functionalized PtRu alloys as labels
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Transforming C60 molecules into graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical DNA sensor based on three-dimensional folding paper device for specific and sensitive point-of-care testing
Microstructured reactors for multiphase reactions: state of the art
Controlled nucleation for the regulation of the particle size in monodisperse gold suspensions
Strongly green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications
Polymer photovoltaic cells based on solution-processable graphene and P3HT
Ionic-liquid-doped polyaniline inverse opals: preparation, characterization, and application for the electrochemical impedance immunoassay of hepatitis B surface antigen
Multiplexed sandwich immunoassays using flow-injection electrochemiluminescence with designed substrate spatial-resolved technique for detection of tumor markers
Electrochemiluminescence of blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots and its application in ultrasensitive aptasensor for adenosine triphosphate detection

Magnetic core-shell Fe3O4@Ag nanoparticles coated carbon paste interface for studies of carcinoembryonic antigen in clinical immunoassay
Flow-injection chemiluminescent immunoassay for α-fetoprotein based on epoxysilane modified glass microbeads
Positive potential operation of a cathodic electrogenerated chemiluminescence immunosensor based on luminol and graphene for cancer biomarker detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Li studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Weiping Li studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Chao Ma studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Hongmei Yang studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.

Shenguang Ge received his PhD degree in Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in 2013 from Shandong University, completed his master degree studies in University of Jinan in 2006. He joined University of Jinan, where currently he is a lecturer of chemistry. His research interests are in the area of biosensor and chemsensor.

Jinghua Yu received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry in 2003 from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, China. She is currently positioned as a professor at University of Jinan. She spends most of her time investigating biomedical engineering, especially for the development of biosensor devices and analytical tools.
